title: LZ78 Complexity Sentiment Classification Experiment with Baseline Comparisons
layman_summary: Tests whether text complexity (measured by LZ78 compression algorithm) can predict sentiment in movie reviews, product reviews, and tweets, comparing against standard sentiment analysis tools.
summary: Implemented and evaluated LZ78 complexity-based sentiment classification across three standardized datasets (IMDB movies, Amazon Polarity products, TweetEval tweets) with baseline comparisons (VADER, TextBlob, NPC-gzip). The experiment includes: (1) LZ78 complexity computation using gzip compressed length as proxy, (2) statistical analysis using Mann-Whitney U test and Cliff's delta effect size, (3) theoretical threshold derivation from information-theoretic first principles (midpoint between class-conditional means), (4) direction-aware classifier that learns whether lower or higher complexity indicates positive sentiment, (5) comprehensive evaluation with accuracy, precision, recall, F1 scores, and confusion matrices, (6) figure generation including complexity distributions and accuracy comparisons. Key findings: LZ78 complexity shows statistically significant but modest correlation with sentiment for Amazon reviews (p<0.001, accuracy 53.2%) and tweets (p<0.001, accuracy 62.9%), but not for IMDB reviews (p=0.316, accuracy ~50% random). Baselines (VADER, TextBlob) achieve 60-75% accuracy. Results suggest LZ78 complexity captures sentiment information in short texts but is less informative for longer reviews.
